function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
The filing discloses two distinct voting mechanisms: (i) Class B shareholders voting as a class to elect a majority of directors, and (ii) combined Class A and Class B voting power determining the remaining board seats and general shareholder matters. Explain precisely how this dual-mechanism structure differs economically and practically from a conventional 10-vote/1-vote dual-class structure in which all directors are elected by the combined class vote. What is the specific implication for a Class A shareholder's ability to influence board composition even if Class A shareholders collectively hold a majority of economic interest?

steps.reasoning_content:
Let me analyze this question carefully. It's about the dual-mechanism voting structure disclosed in the SpaceX S-1 filing, comparing it to a conventional dual-class structure, and understanding the implications for Class A shareholders' ability to influence board composition.

Let me start by retrieving the relevant information from the SpaceX S-1 filing about their voting mechanisms and board election procedures.

results.content: SUCCESS: Loaded portable assets for key: spacex_s1

steps.reasoning_content: Let me now retrieve the relevant information about the voting mechanisms and board election procedures.

arguments.prompt: Describe the two distinct voting mechanisms for electing directors, including the Class B shareholders voting as a class to elect a majority of directors, and the combined Class A and Class B voting power for remaining board seats. Include all details about board composition and voting rights in {{spacex_s1}}.
Describe the capital structure, including Class A, Class B, and Class C shares, their respective voting rights (votes per share), and any other key differences between share classes in {{spacex_s1}}.

results.content:
Based on the provided text, here is a detailed description of the two distinct voting mechanisms for electing directors, along with the associated details about board composition and voting rights.

The company's charter establishes a dual-class stock structure that creates two separate paths for electing the board of directors.

### The Two Voting Mechanisms for Electing Directors

**1. Class B Directors (Majority of the Board)**
The first mechanism grants the holders of Class B common stock the exclusive right to elect a majority of the board. The charter specifies that:
*   **Electing Body:** Holders of Class B common stock, **voting separately as a class**.
*   **Number of Directors:** They are entitled to elect **51% of the total number of authorized directors**, rounded up to the nearest whole number.
*   **Duration:** This right exists **for so long as any shares of Class B common stock remain outstanding**.
*   **Terminology:** Directors elected through this mechanism are referred to as the **“Class B Directors.”**

**2. Common Stock Directors (Remaining Board Seats)**
The second mechanism is used to fill the remaining board seats not elected by the Class B shareholders. The charter specifies that:
*   **Electing Body:** Holders of **all classes of voting common stock (Class A and Class B), voting together as a single class**.
*   **Number of Directors:** They are entitled to elect the **remaining directors** after the Class B directors are seated.
*   **Terminology:** Directors elected through this combined vote are referred to as the **“Common Stock Directors.”**

### Board Composition, Voting Rights, and Related Procedures

The text provides further details on how these two classes of directors are controlled after their election.

*   **Board Size and Practical Control:** Upon the completion of the offering, the board will consist of **eight directors**. The documents state that Elon Musk, through his majority ownership of Class B shares, will be able to elect all the Class B Directors. Furthermore, because he will also hold a majority of the combined voting power of Class A and Class B stock, he will be able to elect the Common Stock Directors as well, giving him the power to control the election of the entire board.

*   **Removal Procedures:**
    *   **Class B Directors:** Can be removed with or without cause by the affirmative vote of the holders of at least a majority of the voting power of the outstanding Class B common stock, voting separately as a class.
    *   **Common Stock Directors:** Can be removed with or without cause by the affirmative vote of the holders of at least a majority of the voting power of the outstanding voting common stock (Class A and B), voting together as a single class.

*   **Vacancy Filling:**
    *   **Class B Director Vacancies:** May be filled by the affirmative vote of the holders of at least a majority of the voting power of the outstanding Class B common stock, voting separately as a class, **or** by the remaining Class B Directors, and not by any other persons.
    *   **Common Stock Director Vacancies:** May be filled by the affirmative vote of the holders of at least a majority of the voting power of the outstanding voting common stock, voting together as a single class, **or** by the remaining directors.

*   **Special Leadership Protections:** The charter provides a special voting right specifically for Elon Musk's leadership roles. His removal from the board and from his positions as **Chief Executive Officer and Chairman of the board** requires the approval of the holders of at least a majority of the voting power of the outstanding Class B common stock, voting separately as a class.

*   **Other Class B Voting Rights:** Beyond director elections, the approval of the Class B common stock, voting separately as a class, is required for any amendment to the charter that would change the rights, powers, preferences, and privileges of the Class B common stock (including with respect to Class B Directors).

*   **Preferred Stock Precedence:** All voting rights described are subject to the rights that may be granted in the future to holders of any series of preferred stock.

Based on the provided document chunks, here is a description of the capital structure for Space Exploration Technologies Corp. (SpaceX), focusing on the Class A, Class B, and Class C common stock.

### Authorized Shares (Post-IPO)

Upon completion of the offering, the authorized capital stock will include:
*   **Class A common stock:** 36,132,150,000 shares, par value $0.001 per share.
*   **Class B common stock:** 6,125,000,000 shares, par value $0.001 per share.
*   **Class C common stock:** 10,000,000,000 shares, par value $0.001 per share. No shares of Class C will be issued and outstanding immediately after the offering.
*   **Preferred stock:** 2,400,000,000 shares, par value $0.001 per share. No shares will be issued and outstanding immediately after the offering.

### Voting Rights (Votes Per Share)

*   **Class A common stock:** Entitled to **one vote per share**.
*   **Class B common stock:** Entitled to **ten votes per share**.
*   **Class C common stock:** Holders have **no voting rights**, except as required by law.

Generally, holders of Class A and Class B common stock vote together as a single class on all matters submitted to shareholders, except as specifically provided in the charter.

### Key Differences Between Share Classes

The primary differences between the share classes are in **voting power and board control**, not in economic rights.

**1. Board of Directors Election Control:**
*   Holders of **Class B common stock**, voting separately as a class, are entitled to elect **51% of the total number of authorized directors** (rounded up to the nearest whole number). This right exists for as long as any shares of Class B common stock remain outstanding.
*   The holders of all classes of voting common stock (Class A and Class B), voting together as a single class, elect the remaining directors.

**2. Protective Provisions for Class B Common Stock:**
*   **Removal of Elon Musk:** The removal of Elon Musk from his roles as Chief Executive Officer and Chairman of the board requires the approval of the holders of at least a majority of the voting power of the outstanding shares of Class B common stock, voting separately as a class.
*   **Removal and Vacancies of Class B Directors:** Class B Directors may only be removed by a majority vote of the outstanding Class B common stock. Vacancies among the Class B Directors may only be filled by a majority vote of the outstanding Class B common stock or by the remaining Class B Directors.
*   **Charter Amendments and Major Transactions:** The approval of the Class B common stock, voting separately as a class, is required to amend the charter in a way that changes the rights, powers, or preferences of the Class B common stock, and to approve certain combinations, mergers, or sales as described in the charter.

**3. Conversion Rights:**
*   **Class B Common Stock:** Each share of Class B common stock is convertible at any time at the option of the holder into **one share of Class A common stock**.
*   **Automatic Conversion of Class B Common Stock:** Each share of Class B common stock will automatically convert into one share of Class A common stock upon a "Transfer" of that share, whether or not for value, subject to exceptions for certain "Permitted Transfers" (e.g., transfers to family members, trusts, and certain related entities for tax or estate planning purposes).
*   **Class A and Class C Common Stock:** Holders of Class A and Class C common stock do not have conversion rights.

**4. Economic Rights (Dividends & Liquidation - Equal):**
*   **Dividends:** Holders of Class A, Class B, and Class C common stock are entitled to receive dividends on a pro rata, equal priority, pari passu basis when declared by the board.
*   **Liquidation:** Upon liquidation, dissolution, or winding up, holders of Class A, Class B, and Class C common stock share ratably in all remaining assets after payments to creditors and any liquidation preferences of outstanding preferred stock.

steps.reasoning_content: Now I have all the key information. Let me also check if there's any disclosure about the specific implications for Class A shareholders regarding their inability to influence the Class B director elections.

arguments.prompt: What risks or implications are disclosed regarding Class A shareholders' limited ability to influence board composition or corporate governance? Include any discussion about the controlled company status, inability to remove Class B directors, and concentrated voting power in {{spacex_s1}}.

results.content:
Based on the provided text, the following risks and implications are disclosed regarding Class A shareholders' limited ability to influence board composition and corporate governance:

### 1. Controlled Company Status and Governance Exemptions
The filings repeatedly disclose that the company will be a "controlled company" under Nasdaq rules due to Elon Musk's majority voting power. The key implication is that the company will rely on exemptions from corporate governance requirements, resulting in fewer protections for Class A shareholders.

*   **Exemptions Utilized:** The company intends not to have a majority-independent board, an independent nominating committee, or a fully independent compensation committee, and it may not conduct annual evaluations of these committees.
*   **Direct Statement of Risk:** The text explicitly states, "Accordingly, our Class A shareholders will not have the same protections afforded to shareholders of companies that are subject to all of the corporate governance requirements of Nasdaq and Nasdaq Texas."

### 2. Inability to Remove Class B Directors
The dual-class structure grants exclusive rights to Class B shareholders to control the election and removal of a majority of the board, effectively precluding Class A shareholders from influencing these outcomes.

*   **Election and Removal of Class B Directors:** The charter gives holders of Class B common stock, voting separately, the right to elect 51% of the board. These "Class B Directors" can only be removed "by the affirmative vote of the holders of at least a majority of the voting power of the outstanding shares of Class B common stock, voting separately as a class."
*   **Removal of Elon Musk:** The text specifies that removing Elon Musk from his board and leadership roles (CEO and Chairman) "requires the approval of the holders of at least a majority of the voting power of the outstanding shares of Class B common stock, voting separately as a class."

### 3. Concentrated Voting Power
The documents detail how Elon Musk's control over a majority of Class B shares gives him decisive power over all matters requiring shareholder approval, limiting Class A shareholders' influence.

*   **Control Over All Directors:** The text states that because Mr. Musk will beneficially own a majority of the voting power, he "will be able to elect all the members of our board." This includes the "Common Stock Directors," who are nominally elected by all shareholders voting together but are effectively controlled by his majority vote.
*   **Control Over Corporate Matters:** Mr. Musk’s concentrated voting power allows him to "control the outcome of matters requiring shareholder approval," which may "have the effect of deferring, delaying or discouraging hostile takeovers, or changes in control or management, of the Company."
*   **Long-Term Control:** The structure is designed to perpetuate this control. The conversion mechanism for Class B shares will, over time, "increas[e] the relative voting power of those holders of Class B common stock who retain their shares."
*   **Risk of Control Shifts:** A specific risk is disclosed that if Mr. Musk sells enough Class B shares to lose his majority of that class, "another holder or group of holders of Class B common stock could obtain the ability to elect and remove a majority of our board and thereby effectively control the Company," leading to changes that "may not be aligned with the interests of holders of our Class A common stock."
*   **Charter Opt-Outs:** The company's charter opts out of separate class votes under Texas law for matters like increasing authorized shares or reclassifications, further entrenching the power of the controlling shareholder.

steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have all the information needed to provide a comprehensive answer. Let me synthesize this.

Key points for the answer:

1. The dual-mechanism structure:
   - Mechanism (i): Class B shareholders, voting separately as a class, elect 51% of directors (rounded up) = 5 out of 8 directors (Class B Directors)
   - Mechanism (ii): Class A and Class B shareholders, voting together as a single class, elect the remaining directors = 3 out of 8 directors (Common Stock Directors)

2. Conventional dual-class structure (e.g., 10-vote/1-vote):
   - All directors are elected by the combined vote of all shareholders
   - A holder with majority voting power controls all seats
   - But in theory, if economic interest became sufficiently dispersed, someone with majority economic interest could also have majority voting power

3. The key economic and practical difference:
   - In the conventional structure, voting power and economic interest are tethered - a shareholder with majority economic interest might indirectly influence board composition through cumulative voting power. Even a shareholder with less than majority voting power could form coalitions.
   - In SpaceX's structure, the Class B separate-class vote creates a firewall: Class A shareholders have ZERO ability to vote on the majority of the board seats (5 of 8), regardless of how much economic interest they hold.

4. Specific implication for Class A shareholders:
   - Even if Class A shareholders collectively hold a majority of economic interest, they cannot vote for or against Class B Directors at all. The Class B Directors are elected exclusively by Class B shareholders voting separately.
   - The only board seats Class A shareholders can influence are the 3 Common Stock Director seats. But even these are effectively controlled by Elon Musk because he holds a majority of the combined voting power.
   - This means Class A shareholders can never achieve board control through accumulation of shares, unlike in a conventional dual-class structure where accumulating enough Class A shares (or buying Class B shares that convert) could eventually yield board influence.

Let me now also quantify the math. 

Board size: 8 directors
Class B Directors: 5 (51% of 8, rounded up)
Common Stock Directors: 3

Even if Class A shareholders collectively hold, say, 90% of the economic interest, they get zero votes on the 5 Class B Director seats. They only participate in the election of 3 Common Stock Directors, where Elon Musk's combined voting power still dominates.

Let me calculate: if Elon Musk holds 85.1% voting power pre-offering, and Class A shareholders collectively hold the remainder, the Class A shareholders cannot elect any Common Stock Directors on their own either - Musk controls the outcome.

Now let me submit the answer.

arguments.final_result:
## The Dual-Mechanism Voting Structure vs. Conventional Dual-Class

### SpaceX's Structure: Two Separate Election Pathways

The SpaceX charter creates two distinct director-election mechanisms:

| Mechanism | Electing Body | Seats |
|---|---|---|
| **Class B Directors** | Class B shareholders **voting separately as a class** | 51% of authorized directors, rounded up (5 of 8 post-IPO) |
| **Common Stock Directors** | Class A and Class B shareholders **voting together as a single class** | Remaining seats (3 of 8 post-IPO) |

Class B shares carry **10 votes per share**; Class A shares carry **1 vote per share**. Elon Musk beneficially owns **93.6%** of Class B stock and holds **85.1%** of combined voting power pre-offering.

### The Critical Structural Difference

In a **conventional 10-vote/1-vote dual-class structure** (such as Meta's or Alphabet's), **all directors are elected by the combined vote of all shareholders voting together**. Even if a founder holds majority voting power through super-voting stock, every share class participates in every director election. The lever of control is purely arithmetic: the super-voting class outvotes the ordinary class in each contest.

In SpaceX's structure, the Class B separate-class vote operates as a **governance firewall**, not merely a voting multiplier. The election of Class B Directors (a board majority) is **categorically removed** from the universe of matters on which Class A shareholders may vote. This distinction is qualitative, not quantitative.

### Economic and Practical Implications

**1. Class A shareholders have zero franchise over a board majority.** Regardless of how much Class A stock they accumulate — even 100% of Class A shares, representing any level of economic interest — Class A shareholders cannot cast a single vote for or against any Class B Director. Those five seats are elected in an election to which Class A shareholders are not invited.

**2. The removal mechanism reinforces the barrier.** Class B Directors can be removed only by a majority vote of Class B shareholders voting separately as a class. Class A shareholders have no removal power over these directors. Similarly, Elon Musk's removal as CEO and Chairman requires separate Class B class approval.

**3. Even the Common Stock Director seats are not practically contestable.** Because Elon Musk holds majority combined voting power (85.1%), he also controls the election of the three Common Stock Directors. The filing explicitly acknowledges he "will be able to elect all the members of our board."

**4. Vacancy-filling entrenches the structure.** Class B Director vacancies are filled either by the remaining Class B Directors or by a separate Class B shareholder vote — never by the full board or combined shareholder vote.

### The Specific Implication: Majority Economic Interest ≠ Any Board Influence

The most analytically significant implication is this: **even if Class A shareholders collectively hold a majority of the economic interest in SpaceX, they have no mechanism to influence the composition of the board majority.**

In a conventional dual-class structure, if public shareholders accumulated sufficient high-vote stock (or if enough Class B shares converted to Class A upon transfer), they could eventually amass enough combined voting power to influence or determine all director elections. The economic interest and voting power, while disproportionate, remain tethered within a single electoral framework.

Under SpaceX's structure, that tether is severed for the board majority. A Class A shareholder could own 99% of the company's economic value and still have **zero votes** on five of eight board seats. The only path to influence over the Class B Director cohort is to own Class B shares — shares overwhelmingly concentrated in Elon Musk's hands (93.6%) and protected from dilution by transfer-based automatic conversion into Class A.

The filing itself confirms this by noting the company qualifies as a "controlled company" under Nasdaq rules and will exempt itself from independent-board, independent-nominating-committee, and independent-compensation-committee requirements, stating: "Accordingly, our Class A shareholders will not have the same protections afforded to shareholders of companies that are subject to all of the corporate governance requirements of Nasdaq."

results.content:
## The Dual-Mechanism Voting Structure vs. Conventional Dual-Class

### SpaceX's Structure: Two Separate Election Pathways

The SpaceX charter creates two distinct director-election mechanisms:

| Mechanism | Electing Body | Seats |
|---|---|---|
| **Class B Directors** | Class B shareholders **voting separately as a class** | 51% of authorized directors, rounded up (5 of 8 post-IPO) |
| **Common Stock Directors** | Class A and Class B shareholders **voting together as a single class** | Remaining seats (3 of 8 post-IPO) |

Class B shares carry **10 votes per share**; Class A shares carry **1 vote per share**. Elon Musk beneficially owns **93.6%** of Class B stock and holds **85.1%** of combined voting power pre-offering.

### The Critical Structural Difference

In a **conventional 10-vote/1-vote dual-class structure** (such as Meta's or Alphabet's), **all directors are elected by the combined vote of all shareholders voting together**. Even if a founder holds majority voting power through super-voting stock, every share class participates in every director election. The lever of control is purely arithmetic: the super-voting class outvotes the ordinary class in each contest.

In SpaceX's structure, the Class B separate-class vote operates as a **governance firewall**, not merely a voting multiplier. The election of Class B Directors (a board majority) is **categorically removed** from the universe of matters on which Class A shareholders may vote. This distinction is qualitative, not quantitative.

### Economic and Practical Implications

**1. Class A shareholders have zero franchise over a board majority.** Regardless of how much Class A stock they accumulate — even 100% of Class A shares, representing any level of economic interest — Class A shareholders cannot cast a single vote for or against any Class B Director. Those five seats are elected in an election to which Class A shareholders are not invited.

**2. The removal mechanism reinforces the barrier.** Class B Directors can be removed only by a majority vote of Class B shareholders voting separately as a class. Class A shareholders have no removal power over these directors. Similarly, Elon Musk's removal as CEO and Chairman requires separate Class B class approval.

**3. Even the Common Stock Director seats are not practically contestable.** Because Elon Musk holds majority combined voting power (85.1%), he also controls the election of the three Common Stock Directors. The filing explicitly acknowledges he "will be able to elect all the members of our board."

**4. Vacancy-filling entrenches the structure.** Class B Director vacancies are filled either by the remaining Class B Directors or by a separate Class B shareholder vote — never by the full board or combined shareholder vote.

### The Specific Implication: Majority Economic Interest ≠ Any Board Influence

The most analytically significant implication is this: **even if Class A shareholders collectively hold a majority of the economic interest in SpaceX, they have no mechanism to influence the composition of the board majority.**

In a conventional dual-class structure, if public shareholders accumulated sufficient high-vote stock (or if enough Class B shares converted to Class A upon transfer), they could eventually amass enough combined voting power to influence or determine all director elections. The economic interest and voting power, while disproportionate, remain tethered within a single electoral framework.

Under SpaceX's structure, that tether is severed for the board majority. A Class A shareholder could own 99% of the company's economic value and still have **zero votes** on five of eight board seats. The only path to influence over the Class B Director cohort is to own Class B shares — shares overwhelmingly concentrated in Elon Musk's hands (93.6%) and protected from dilution by transfer-based automatic conversion into Class A.

The filing itself confirms this by noting the company qualifies as a "controlled company" under Nasdaq rules and will exempt itself from independent-board, independent-nominating-committee, and independent-compensation-committee requirements, stating: "Accordingly, our Class A shareholders will not have the same protections afforded to shareholders of companies that are subject to all of the corporate governance requirements of Nasdaq."
